                                         <div data-a-expander-name="book_description_expander" data-a-expander-collapsed-height="280" class="a-expander-collapsed-height a-row a-expander-container a-spacing-base a-expander-partial-collapse-container" style="max-height:280px"> <div data-expanded="false" class="a-expander-content a-expander-partial-collapse-content"> <span class="a-text-bold">The historical continuity of spinal catastrophism, traced across multiform encounters between philosophy, psychology, biology, and geology.</span><p><span>Drawing on cryptic intimations in the work of J. G. Ballard, Georges Bataille, William Burroughs, André Leroi-Gourhan, Elaine Morgan, and Friedrich Nietzsche, in the late twentieth century Daniel Barker formulated the axioms of spinal catastrophism: If human morphology, upright posture, and the possibility of language are the ramified accidents of natural history, then psychic ailments are ultimately afflictions of the spine, which itself is a scale model of biogenetic trauma, a portable map of the catastrophic events that shaped that atrocity exhibition of evolutionary traumata, the sick orthograde talking mammal.</span></p><p><span>Tracing its provenance through the biological notions of phylogeny and “organic memory” that fueled early psychoanalysis, back into idealism, nature philosophy, and romanticism, and across multiform encounters between philosophy, psychology, biology, and geology, Thomas Moynihan reveals the historical continuity of spinal catastrophism. From psychoanalysis and myth to geology and neuroanatomy, from bioanalysis to chronopathy, from spinal colonies of proto-minds to the retroparasitism of the CNS, from “railway spine” to Elizabeth Taylor's lost gill-slits, this extravagantly comprehensive philosophical adventure uses the spinal cord as a guiding thread to rediscover forgotten pathways in modern thought. <br/>Moynihan demonstrates that, far from being an fanciful notion rendered obsolete by advances in biology, spinal catastrophism dramatizes fundamental philosophical problematics of time, identity, continuity, and the transcendental that remain central to any attempt to reconcile human experience with natural history.</span></p>  </div> <div class="a-expander-header a-expander-partial-collapse-header"><div class="a-expander-content-fade"></div><a href="javascript:void(0)" role="button" data-action="a-expander-toggle" class="a-declarative" data-a-expander-toggle="{&quot;allowLinkDefault&quot;:true, &quot;expand_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read more&quot;, &quot;collapse_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read less&quot;}"><i class="a-icon a-icon-extender-expand"></i><span class="a-expander-prompt">Read more</span></a></div> </div>                                    </div>

  <script type='text/javascript'>(function ($Nav) {
"use strict";

if (typeof $Nav === 'undefined' || $Nav === null || typeof $Nav.when !== 'function') {
    return;
}

$Nav.when('$', 'data', 'flyout.yourAccount', 'sidepanel.csYourAccount',
          'config')
    .run("BuyitAgain-YourAccount-SidePanel",
    function ($, data, yaFlyout, csYourAccount, config) {
        if (config.disableBuyItAgain) {
            return;
        }
        var render = function (data) {
            if (data.dramResult) {
                var widgetHtml = data.dramResult;
                navbar.sidePanel({
                    flyoutName: 'yourAccount',
                    data: {html: widgetHtml}
                });
            }
        };

        var renderBuyItAgain = function (biaData) {
            if (csYourAccount) {
                csYourAccount.register(render, biaData);
            } else {
                render(biaData);
            }
        };

        var truncateAndRestructureYaFlyout = function() {
            if (window.P) {
                P.when('A', 'a-truncate').execute(function(A, truncate) {
                    var truncateElements = A.$('.a-truncate');
                    A.each(truncateElements, function(element) {
                        truncate.get(element).update();
                    });
                    var recommendationsWidget = document.getElementById('bia-hcb-widget');
                    if (recommendationsWidget) { 
                        var navFlyout = recommendationsWidget.parentElement;
                        var navFlyoutPaddingBottom = parseInt(window.getComputedStyle(navFlyout).getPropertyValue('padding-bottom'));
                        var navFlyoutContentHeight = navFlyout.clientHeight - navFlyoutPaddingBottom;
                        while (recommendationsWidget.offsetHeight > navFlyoutContentHeight && recommendationsWidget.offsetHeight > 0){
                            var recommendations = recommendationsWidget.querySelectorAll('.biaNavFlyoutFaceout');
                            if (recommendations.length <= 1) {
                                break;
                            }
                            var lastRecommendation = recommendations[recommendations.length - 1];
                            lastRecommendation.parentElement.removeChild(lastRecommendation);
                        }
                    }
               });
            }
        };

        yaFlyout.sidePanel.onData(truncateAndRestructureYaFlyout);
        yaFlyout.onShow(truncateAndRestructureYaFlyout);

    yaFlyout.onRender(function() {
            $.ajax({
                url: '/gp/bia/external/bia-hcb-ajax-handler.html',
                data: {"biaHcbRid":"6NYX79B12YK1GV151WH3"},
                dataType: 'json',
                timeout: 4*1000,
                success: renderBuyItAgain,
                error: function (jqXHR, textStatus, errorThrown) {
                }
            });
        });
    });
})(window.$Nav);</script>

them.</p><p>To calculate the overall star rating and percentage breakdown by star, we don’t use a simple average. <span data-hook="review-body" class="a-size-base review-text"><div data-a-expander-name="review_text_read_more" data-a-expander-collapsed-height="300" class="a-expander-collapsed-height a-row a-expander-container a-expander-partial-collapse-container review-text-read-more-expander" style="max-height:300px"><div data-hook="review-collapsed" data-expanded="false" class="a-expander-content reviewText review-text-content a-expander-partial-collapse-content">







  
  
    <span>This is a wild read, and once I let my brain absorb it I’ll probably start reading it again. As it is, the book was entertaining enough for me to blow through it in a weekend (complete with underlining and note taking.) Mixed in with the standard philosophers such as Hegel, Kant and Schopenhauer are a good number of lesser known authors, some of, shall we say, dubious existence. There’s also a healthy dose of fiction (Ballard, the Urbanomic favorite, is a star) and outdated (?) scientific writing to round out the history. All in all, an entertaining and disquieting read.<br /></span>

      <div data-hook="genome-widget" class="a-row a-spacing-mini"><a href="/gp/profile/amzn1.account.AFWIX4XDNLPG6UPSXCYWD5V753KA <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/gp/profile/amzn1.account.AFWIX4XDNLPG6UPSXCYWD5V753KA>" class="a-profile" data-a-size="small"><div aria-hidden="true" class="a-profile-avatar-wrapper"><div class="a-profile-avatar"><img src="https://images-na.ssl-images-amazon.com/images/G/01/x-locale/common/grey-pixel.gif <view-source:https://images-na.ssl-images-amazon.com/images/G/01/x-locale/common/grey-pixel.gif>" class="a-lazy-loaded" data-src="https://m.media-amazon.com/images/S/amazon-avatars-global/default.png"/><noscript><img src="https://m.media-amazon.com/images/S/amazon-avatars-global/default.png"/></noscript></div></div><div class="a-profile-content"><span class="a-profile-name">Andrew</span></div></a></div><div class="a-row"><h5><a data-hook="review-title" class="a-size-base a-link-normal review-title a-color-base review-title-content a-text-bold" href="/gp/customer-reviews/R3CXXLKD5M083O/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_ttl?ie=UTF8 <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/gp/customer-reviews/R3CXXLKD5M083O/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_ttl?ie=UTF8>"><i data-hook="review-star-rating" class="a-icon a-icon-star a-star-2 review-rating"><span class="a-icon-alt">2.0 out of 5 stars</span></i><span class="a-letter-space"></span>







  
  
    <span>Outdated and misrepresented</span>
  
</a></h5></div><span data-hook="review-date" aria-level="6" class="a-size-base a-color-secondary review-date" role="heading">Reviewed in the United States on June 1, 2021</span><div class="a-row a-spacing-mini review-data review-format-strip"><span data-hook="format-strip-linkless" class="a-color-secondary">Format: Paperback</span><i aria-hidden="true" class="a-icon a-icon-text-separator" role="img" aria-label="|"></i><a aria-label="Verified Purchase: Learn more how customers reviews work on Amazon" class="a-link-normal" target="_blank" rel="noopener" href="/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA>"><span data-hook="avp-badge" class="a-size-mini a-color-state a-text-bold">Verified Purchase</span></a></div><div class="a-row a-spacing-small review-data"><span data-hook="review-body" class="a-size-base review-text"><div data-a-expander-name="review_text_read_more" data-a-expander-collapsed-height="300" class="a-expander-collapsed-height a-row a-expander-container a-expander-partial-collapse-container review-text-read-more-expander" style="max-height:300px"><div data-hook="review-collapsed" data-expanded="false" class="a-expander-content reviewText review-text-content a-expander-partial-collapse-content">







  
  
    <span>I find the topic of lost knowledge and the re-evaluation of any data contained within, let’s say, intriguing. The Nietzschean/Foucauldian route of approaching this work as a genealogy, or as the author likes to describe, “hypergenealogy” partucularly piqued my interest. Suffice it to say, the authors anthropocentric attitude and humanist view sully this approach. By delving into “lost knowledge” we venture to the unknown philosophers of: Kant, Hegel, and Marx. The author, taking a few skips along the path, leaps to the 1970’s to discuss the musings of the mad as “philosophical” conjecture. Honestly disappointed, Moynihan should follow the thread of Nietzsche and Foucault and discover, Saint Max Stirner. Stirner pre-dating the two fore-mentioned thinkers would refute the German Idealists (as well as all ideology, religion, or fixed idea; a spook as Stirner would say) wholesale and his work has yet to receive a valid philosophical response.<br /></span>
  
</div><div class="a-expander-header a-expander-partial-collapse-header"><div class="a-expander-content-fade"></div><a href="javascript:void(0)" data-hook="expand-collapse-read-more-less" aria-label="Read more of this review" aria-expanded="false" role="button" data-action="a-expander-toggle" class="a-declarative" data-a-expander-toggle="{&quot;allowLinkDefault&quot;:true, &quot;expand_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read more&quot;, &quot;collapse_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read less&quot;}"><i class="a-icon a-icon-extender-expand"></i><span class="a-expander-prompt">Read more</span></a></div></div></span></div><div class="a-row cr-vote-action-bar"><span class="cr-vote" data-hook="review-voting-widget">

        <span><i aria-hidden="true" class="a-icon a-icon-text-separator" role="img" aria-label="|"></i><span class="a-declarative" data-action="reviews:report-abusive-review-modal-button" data-reviews:report-abusive-review-modal-button="{&quot;closeButton&quot;:&quot;true&quot;,&quot;name&quot;:&quot;report-abusive-review-modal-popover-R3CXXLKD5M083O&quot;,&quot;width&quot;:&quot;350&quot;,&quot;header&quot;:&quot;Report this review&quot;,&quot;url&quot;:&quot;/hz/reviews-render/report-review/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_report?csrfT=hKfj9BW8I7+Vbp9yJIhblqY7GbogAWr2zWDyl6D/7mUqAAAAAGmwj9kAAAAB&amp;reviewId=R3CXXLKD5M083O&quot;}" id="report-abusive-review-modal-popover-R3CXXLKD5M083O"><a aria-label="Report review by Andrew" class="a-size-base a-link-normal a-color-secondary report-abuse-link a-text-normal" href="/hz/reviews-render/report-review/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_report?csrfT=hKfj9BW8I7+Vbp9yJIhblqY7GbogAWr2zWDyl6D/7mUqAAAAAGmwj9kAAAAB&amp;reviewId=R3CXXLKD5M083O <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/hz/reviews-render/report-review/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_report?csrfT=hKfj9BW8I7+Vbp9yJIhblqY7GbogAWr2zWDyl6D/7mUqAAAAAGmwj9kAAAAB&reviewId=R3CXXLKD5M083O>">Report</a></span></span></span>

      <div data-hook="genome-widget" class="a-row a-spacing-mini"><a href="/gp/profile/amzn1.account.AFWGFBVGBXTVOO5GH62VI4G5I7IA <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/gp/profile/amzn1.account.AFWGFBVGBXTVOO5GH62VI4G5I7IA>" class="a-profile" data-a-size="small"><div aria-hidden="true" class="a-profile-avatar-wrapper"><div class="a-profile-avatar"><img src="https://images-na.ssl-images-amazon.com/images/G/01/x-locale/common/grey-pixel.gif <view-source:https://images-na.ssl-images-amazon.com/images/G/01/x-locale/common/grey-pixel.gif>" class="a-lazy-loaded" data-src="https://m.media-amazon.com/images/S/amazon-avatars-global/9dcdafc0-f6dd-440b-a347-0821ec67b1fb._CR45%2C0%2C409%2C409_SX460_.jpg"/><noscript><img src="https://m.media-amazon.com/images/S/amazon-avatars-global/9dcdafc0-f6dd-440b-a347-0821ec67b1fb._CR45%2C0%2C409%2C409_SX460_.jpg"/></noscript></div></div><div class="a-profile-content"><span class="a-profile-name">Anthony Simeone</span></div></a></div><div class="a-row"><h5><a data-hook="review-title" class="a-size-base a-link-normal review-title a-color-base review-title-content a-text-bold" href="/gp/customer-reviews/R6LP87RLWRE54/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_ttl?ie=UTF8 <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/gp/customer-reviews/R6LP87RLWRE54/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_ttl?ie=UTF8>"><i data-hook="review-star-rating" class="a-icon a-icon-star a-star-1 review-rating"><span class="a-icon-alt">1.0 out of 5 stars</span></i><span class="a-letter-space"></span>







  
  
    <span>Somebody read too much cosmic horror...</span>
  
</a></h5></div><span data-hook="review-date" aria-level="6" class="a-size-base a-color-secondary review-date" role="heading">Reviewed in the United States on September 1, 2024</span><div class="a-row a-spacing-mini review-data review-format-strip"><span data-hook="format-strip-linkless" class="a-color-secondary">Format: Paperback</span></div><div class="a-row a-spacing-small review-data"><span data-hook="review-body" class="a-size-base review-text"><div data-a-expander-name="review_text_read_more" data-a-expander-collapsed-height="300" class="a-expander-collapsed-height a-row a-expander-container a-expander-partial-collapse-container review-text-read-more-expander" style="max-height:300px"><div data-hook="review-collapsed" data-expanded="false" class="a-expander-content reviewText review-text-content a-expander-partial-collapse-content">







  
  
    <span>What if Rust from True Detective season 1 wrote a pseudoscience, pseudophilosophy treatise? Look no further than Spinal Catastrophism! It reads like the ultimate edgelord screed espousing some kind of &#34;evolutionary/biological nihilism.&#34; Just read the following excerpt:<br /><br />&#34;...life is just one prolonged hypnogogic jerk, and, accordingly, the colossal malignancy of existence itself becomes merely an arrhythmic belatedness or precociousness relative to non-existence’s obsidian repose: a vast, drawn-out chronopathy.&#34;<br /><br />If Lovecraft were still alive, he might read that and faint with rapture! Yes, folks: life, nay, the human being itself, is a malignancy! Forget about just being accidental. You&#39;re actually a bipedal tumor in the colon of the cosmos!<br /><br />Of course the darling of the armchair nihilist makes an appearance: Nietzsche! Ooh, that means it&#39;s real edgy, people! Oh look, there&#39;s Freud, too! He&#39;ll definitely help boil human motivations down to weird sexual obsessions. Hey, did you know guys like Napoleon instigated wars because humans still have lizard brains deep down? Definitely not because of human-made systems of government and society rife with in-built disparity and injustice! It&#39;s the biological imperative, yo!<br /><br />Read this book if you want another exhausting slog through a crackpot stew,  made with a whole bunch of obsolete, debunked, elitist ideas from the last few centuries thrown into the seething cauldron. Spice it up with ideas from actual science fiction (JG Ballard is one &#34;source&#34; used in the tome) and you got yourself one hearty jambalaya of pseudointellectual tripe trimmed with all the big, fancy, arcane, obscure words you can stomach!<br /><br />But of you want the TLDR, lemme sum it up for you: people suck because we evolved that way. What a novel concept! I definitely haven&#39;t heard that from endless Youtube videos about so-called stoicism, cynicism, evolutionary psychology grifts, etc. We&#39;re inherently terrible and that&#39;s all, dontchaknow? Just ignore the current unprecedented level of affluence of most first world countries, which gives people like Moynihan the luxury to LARP in his own imaginary grimdark version of history and human evolution. Our ancestors who lived short and brutish lives would weep at how we squander our privilege and plenty with this type of useless speculation and navel-gazing, as we waste our good fortune by insisting on believing the worst about our species.<br /><br />Honestly, it&#39;s books like this that make me wonder if the religious folks are right when they say we need faith in a God to be moral beings. If atheism enables this sort of empty ideological word salad, gimme that old time religion!<br /><br />Seriously, if you want to read something philosophical with a little hope for, and belief in, humanity, go read Capra&#39;s The Turning Point, or Jonas Salk&#39;s A New Reality, or Rifkin&#39;s The Empathic Civilization. Leave the Spinal Catastrophism drivel on the ash heap of history with all the rest of the &#34;cynicism is cool&#34; dross.<br /></span>
  
</div><div class="a-expander-header a-expander-partial-collapse-header"><div class="a-expander-content-fade"></div><a href="javascript:void(0)" data-hook="expand-collapse-read-more-less" aria-label="Read more of this review" aria-expanded="false" role="button" data-action="a-expander-toggle" class="a-declarative" data-a-expander-toggle="{&quot;allowLinkDefault&quot;:true, &quot;expand_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read more&quot;, &quot;collapse_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read less&quot;}"><i class="a-icon a-icon-extender-expand"></i><span class="a-expander-prompt">Read more</span></a></div></div></span></div><div class="a-row cr-vote-action-bar"><span class="cr-vote" data-hook="review-voting-widget">

    <span>I wanted to like this book</span>
  
</a></h5></div><span data-hook="review-date" aria-level="6" class="a-size-base a-color-secondary review-date" role="heading">Reviewed in the United States on May 31, 2022</span><div class="a-row a-spacing-mini review-data review-format-strip"><span data-hook="format-strip-linkless" class="a-color-secondary">Format: Paperback</span><i aria-hidden="true" class="a-icon a-icon-text-separator" role="img" aria-label="|"></i><a aria-label="Verified Purchase: Learn more how customers reviews work on Amazon" class="a-link-normal" target="_blank" rel="noopener" href="/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA>"><span data-hook="avp-badge" class="a-size-mini a-color-state a-text-bold">Verified Purchase</span></a></div><div class="a-row a-spacing-small review-data"><span data-hook="review-body" class="a-size-base review-text"><div data-a-expander-name="review_text_read_more" data-a-expander-collapsed-height="300" class="a-expander-collapsed-height a-row a-expander-container a-expander-partial-collapse-container review-text-read-more-expander" style="max-height:300px"><div data-hook="review-collapsed" data-expanded="false" class="a-expander-content reviewText review-text-content a-expander-partial-collapse-content">







  
  
    <span>But I don’t like having to read with a dictionary. And most of the time, not even that helps, with the word salad of hyperstitions and constant use of portmanteau. At times it seems not even the author knows what he’s talking about. I know he doesn’t speak like this, so wtf?<br /></span>

    <span>Outstanding, with a caveat</span>
  
</span></h5></div><span data-hook="review-date" aria-level="6" class="a-size-base a-color-secondary review-date" role="heading">Reviewed in Germany on September 14, 2020</span><div class="a-row a-spacing-mini review-data review-format-strip"><span data-hook="format-strip-linkless" class="a-color-secondary">Format: Paperback</span><i aria-hidden="true" class="a-icon a-icon-text-separator" role="img" aria-label="|"></i><a aria-label="Verified Purchase: Learn more how customers reviews work on Amazon" class="a-link-normal" target="_blank" rel="noopener" href="/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA>"><span data-hook="avp-badge" class="a-size-mini a-color-state a-text-bold">Verified Purchase</span></a></div><div class="a-row a-spacing-small review-data"><script>

<span data-hook="review-body" class="a-size-base review-text"><div data-a-expander-name="review_text_read_more" data-a-expander-collapsed-height="300" class="a-expander-collapsed-height a-row a-expander-container a-expander-partial-collapse-container review-text-read-more-expander" style="max-height:300px"><div data-hook="review-collapsed" data-expanded="false" class="a-expander-content reviewText review-text-content a-expander-partial-collapse-content">







  
  
    <span>It&#39;s impossible to give an adequate summary in a single review. The reader should know that Moynihan uses a special brand of esoteric fictionalism (as another commenter mentioned) which conflates existing documents with fictional texts. As long as you keep this in mind, this will be an exhilarating read.<br /></span>
  
</div><div class="a-expander-header a-expander-partial-collapse-header"><div class="a-expander-content-fade"></div><a href="javascript:void(0)" data-hook="expand-collapse-read-more-less" aria-label="Read more of this review" aria-expanded="false" role="button" data-action="a-expander-toggle" class="a-declarative" data-a-expander-toggle="{&quot;allowLinkDefault&quot;:true, &quot;expand_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read more&quot;, &quot;collapse_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read less&quot;}"><i class="a-icon a-icon-extender-expand"></i><span class="a-expander-prompt">Read more</span></a></div></div></span></div><div class="a-row cr-vote-action-bar"><span class="cr-vote" data-hook="review-voting-widget">

      <div data-hook="genome-widget" class="a-row a-spacing-mini"><div class="a-profile" data-a-size="small"><div aria-hidden="true" class="a-profile-avatar-wrapper"><div class="a-profile-avatar"><img src="https://images-na.ssl-images-amazon.com/images/G/01/x-locale/common/grey-pixel.gif <view-source:https://images-na.ssl-images-amazon.com/images/G/01/x-locale/common/grey-pixel.gif>" class="a-lazy-loaded" data-src="https://m.media-amazon.com/images/S/amazon-avatars-global/default.png"/><noscript><img src="https://m.media-amazon.com/images/S/amazon-avatars-global/default.png"/></noscript></div></div><div class="a-profile-content"><span class="a-profile-name">Jean Alves Catarino</span></div></div></div><div class="a-row a-spacing-none"><h5><i data-hook="cmps-review-star-rating" class="a-icon a-icon-star a-star-5 review-rating"><span class="a-icon-alt">5.0 out of 5 stars</span></i><span class="a-letter-space"></span><span data-hook="review-title" class="a-size-base review-title a-color-base review-title-content a-text-bold">







  
  
    <span>Spiritual &#34;Sequel&#34; to Ligotti&#39;s Conspiracy Against the Human Race</span>
  
</span></h5></div><span data-hook="review-date" aria-level="6" class="a-size-base a-color-secondary review-date" role="heading">Reviewed in Brazil on July 27, 2022</span><div class="a-row a-spacing-mini review-data review-format-strip"><span data-hook="format-strip-linkless" class="a-color-secondary">Format: Paperback</span><i aria-hidden="true" class="a-icon a-icon-text-separator" role="img" aria-label="|"></i><a aria-label="Verified Purchase: Learn more how customers reviews work on Amazon" class="a-link-normal" target="_blank" rel="noopener" href="/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA>"><span data-hook="avp-badge" class="a-size-mini a-color-state a-text-bold">Verified Purchase</span></a></div><div class="a-row a-spacing-small review-data"><span data-hook="review-body" class="a-size-base review-text"><div data-a-expander-name="review_text_read_more" data-a-expander-collapsed-height="300" class="a-expander-collapsed-height a-row a-expander-container a-expander-partial-collapse-container review-text-read-more-expander" style="max-height:300px"><div data-hook="review-collapsed" data-expanded="false" class="a-expander-content reviewText review-text-content a-expander-partial-collapse-content">







  
  
    <span>What begins as a rather cryptic and nearly indecipherable reading (“psychosomatic containment of oneself, when percolated through grandest history, equals hypogene alienation - the alienation of a body riddled with time. It is this realisation that is inaugural of the phylogenetic phantasy that is Spinal Catastrophism”), develops into a bizarre but scientifically grounded thesis that ends up corroborating some of the fundamental philosophical pessimism tenets (“life is just one prolonged hypnogogic jerk, and, accordingly, the colossal malignancy of existence itself becomes merely an arrhythmic belatedness or precociousness relative to non-existence’s obsidian repose: a vast, drawn-out chronopathy”). Even though it might reek of pseudoscience at some points, it feels like what a sequel to The Conspiracy Against The Human Race would look like, coupled with a conclusion that seems to ratify Mainlander’s will-to-death concept and mythos.<br /><br />Some more quotes:<br /><br />“When presented with an infection such as a brain, eudemonia and euthanasia converge.”<br /><br />“The spine is nothing but the symptomatology of the parasitism called existence.”<br /><br />“It is the duty of a spine to destroy the universe; or, a spine is the universe’s method of acknowledging this duty to self-destruct.”<br /></span>
  
</div><div class="a-expander-header a-expander-partial-collapse-header"><div class="a-expander-content-fade"></div><a href="javascript:void(0)" data-hook="expand-collapse-read-more-less" aria-label="Read more of this review" aria-expanded="false" role="button" data-action="a-expander-toggle" class="a-declarative" data-a-expander-toggle="{&quot;allowLinkDefault&quot;:true, &quot;expand_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read more&quot;, &quot;collapse_prompt&quot;:&quot;Read less&quot;}"><i class="a-icon a-icon-extender-expand"></i><span class="a-expander-prompt">Read more</span></a></div></div></span></div><div class="a-row cr-vote-action-bar"><span class="cr-vote" data-hook="review-voting-widget">
        </span>

    <span>Intriguing, insightful, must read if you’re into down the rabbit hole philosophy !</span>
  
</span></h5></div><span data-hook="review-date" aria-level="6" class="a-size-base a-color-secondary review-date" role="heading">Reviewed in Canada on February 15, 2020</span><div class="a-row a-spacing-mini review-data review-format-strip"><span data-hook="format-strip-linkless" class="a-color-secondary">Format: Paperback</span><i aria-hidden="true" class="a-icon a-icon-text-separator" role="img" aria-label="|"></i><a aria-label="Verified Purchase: Learn more how customers reviews work on Amazon" class="a-link-normal" target="_blank" rel="noopener" href="/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA <view-source:https://www.amazon.com/gp/help/customer/display.html/ref=cm_cr_dp_d_rvw_avp?nodeId=G8UYX7LALQC8V9KA>"><span data-hook="avp-badge" class="a-size-mini a-color-state a-text-bold">Verified Purchase</span></a></div><div class="a-row a-spacing-small review-data"><span data-hook="review-body" class="a-size-base review-text"><div data-a-expander-name="review_text_read_more" data-a-expander-collapsed-height="300" class="a-expander-collapsed-height a-row a-expander-container a-expander-partial-collapse-container review-text-read-more-expander" style="max-height:300px"><div data-hook="review-collapsed" data-expanded="false" class="a-expander-content reviewText review-text-content a-expander-partial-collapse-content">







  
  
    <span>Interesting title from Urbanomic. The topic of ‘Spinal Catastrophism’ comes from hyperstitional entity: “D.C Barker”. This was generated by the CCRU and Nick Land back in the 90’s. Funny how the book doesn’t explain this but simply latches onto it and explores the topic in detail. Otherwise solid book.<br /></span>
